commentBody:
App has crashed seven times tonight. Been crashing like once per day the past week. I deleted and re=installed. 

Anyone?
Done, but I'm still confused about one answer.

I may be first out here today. A rarity for me. 

On your wavelength today, John!
This page;s URL should be: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/29/crosswords/daily-puzzle-2026-06-30.html
Hope somebody opens the front door...

Our constructor writes:
"... I especially love that it packs five theme entries plus a revealer into the grid."

And well you should, John. Fine debut!
(These are not EMPTY WORDS.)
I keep getting kicked out of the app — Pad 9 iOS 26.5

Happy to give a big pat on the back to a debut constructor from my home state of Wisconsin and the wonderful city of Madison where I went to medical school. John, this is a superb Tuesday for anyone,  not just for a debut. Five themers crossed and a revealer, plus a lot of decent additional fills (HOGCALLS, OUTSMART, POWERNAP), and very little glue is a (M)ajor  (T)riumph on any day of the week for any constructor. I did figure the theme out early which was great to help suss out the long clues without crosses. My only hiccup today was the rather green-painty ALEGLASS (is there such a thing? I’ve heard of beer glasses?) but readily forgivable in an otherwise pretty impeccable grid. Great job, John, looking forward to more from you soon. Say hi to Lincoln’s statue for me when you pass by and let me know if he stands up.

The clue for POWER NAP made me wonder about the unit of the “wink”. Is a person capable of 3 winks/min a better power napper than a slacker bumbling along at 1.2 winks/min?
If you increase your sleep velocity, are you then able to get your daily quota of winks in a shorter time?
Is there a terminal velocity, beyond which you are sleeping so fast that you actually wake up more tired?
So many questions.

From a cluing perspective, shouldn’t 38A include an indication that the answer is an abbreviation? ADOUT is the abbreviated form of “Advantage out” but nothing in the clue indicates we are looking for an abbreviation.

I had ABORT in there for some time before the downs cleared it up,

Puzzle in a hurry here, coming through! 
Right off the top, it's SLAM, then WHOA. At 10A it's ONEDGE, then (23A) AT LARGE, only to escape on MISTERTOAD's Wild Ride. 
It'll OUTSMART you and then (47A) it's PASTEd its way out and SPED away. 
Need a POWERNAP? I do.
The link on the Games page is still broken 90 minutes after 10:00 pm ET.

Masterful Tuesday.

I was even thinking I might blue star this puzzle, which is in effect a compliment to the constructor!

Congratulations on a fine debut, Mr. Liber, and thank you for a wonderful ride.

Love the continuation Valley Talk with Julia Stiles.

Fair Tuesday.  

And also, Kelsey Pfendler should be closing in on 250 nautical miles soon.  She’s running low on coffee. Hopefully she gets to Oahu before she’s out of caffeine! Hope she sets a new world record.  Row, Kelsey, Row!
Can someone explain “adout”, 38A?

Yay me! I did this one using only the Across clues (without peeking at the Downs) in 21:33. I do this not to torture myself but just to add a bit of extra challenge and to eke a bit more fun out of the puzzles that are a bit easier early in the week. 

This was lovely puzzle, with no funny business and no nonsense. Once i figured out the “MT” WORDS, i got a huge boost — suddenly some inaccessible down clues opened up their secrets. I got lucky with interpolating guesses for M__HIN_T___S and M_V__T_E_TER and the rest was a joy! 

Thank you John Liber, great debut!
A nicely Toughened Up Tuesday, I thought. Caught onto the theme about midway, which helped complete some of the themers. And any puzzle that reminds me of ANSEL's magnicent works is OKay by me.
My Thoughts: Clever and enjoyable Tuesday theme. My favorite clue was for 'Hog calls'. Made me smile when answer appeared through crossings. Ready for more. Maybe Tomorrow?
Second tough Tuesday in a row!! Are we creating a new trend??

This puzzle had me scratching my head, trying to think of real language examples: "Buck excelled not only in nag calls like "Whoa, Nellie!", but also in hog calls." "The bartender told the barback to put the ale glasses on the top shelf, and the lager glasses on the shelf below." Is there a difference? Apparently so: https://www.kegworks.com/blog/beer-glassware-guide-beer-glass-types-uses* Also, back when one was so bullocksy as to use "mad" as a verb, you were usually making someone insane, or amazed, not angry: "Thei leueden hym, for long tyme he hadde maddid hem with his witche craftis."--Acts 8:11, Wycliffe--"he" being, apparently, Simon Magus. Usage dropped off pretty quick after that, but people still madded one another in seaside England until the early 19th c. (George Crabbe, "The Borough"), and in pulp westerns until the 1920's: "O' course, it ain't that any of them's afraid to mad that crazy gunman, Tait." (Grateful thanks to the Oxford English Dictionary, and to Ctrl-V.) And I thought that the Hatter, whom mercury poisoning had madded long since, perhaps collected TICKETs ("This STILE 10/6"), not TEA SETs. Or maybe weird, anthropomorphic friends. (I guess one, singular tea set constitutes a collection. *https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wdJeaNnNG4c

The unusual pattern of the thematic entries made this Tuesday puzzle feel a bit different, in a good way. The theme was clever enough, and there were some interesting non-thematic entries: MILADY, HOGCALLS, GODARK, POWERNAP.

WIIU looked very strange, and MADS clued as a verb was new to me. Would "Scandanavian boy name" have been worse?

Liked this puzzle, and solved it much faster than yesterday's---though using both the horizontal and vertical clues really felt like cheating.  ;-)

Thought it was a tricky Tuesday for a minute.

Then sussed out that I had used the 
oft-confused alternate spelling
of PHONE.


PnONE.

I call natick.
IONE Skye yesterday, IONIA today.  All we need is IONA University tomorrow.
MANYTHANKS for the fine debut.

Excellent debut! It was a real treat to see the crisscrossing of theme entries. It took what could have been a regular Tuesday and made it a bit more interesting. We need more thinking outside the box like this!

After several "Page Not Found" messages and a trip on the merry-go-round, I'm here to congratulate the new constructor...but regretfully report that I found this Mighty Tedious, although I put the blame on the fact that it's Merely Tuesday...  

The weather-folk here are bemoaning what seems to us just normal Mississippi Tropical weather--in other words, Hell.  Temp of 96, high humidity, for a total "feels-like" of 102.  
Another batch of local politicos is off to the slammer.....for various crimes filed under Money Talks.  

Bye for now...More Tomorrow!

Does anyone else’s iOS app now have Wordle at the top and crossword really far down the list? If you’re going to do this, make it customizable. Otherwise I absolutely hate this update and am going to be really annoyed by it every single day.

Bit of a Tuesday workout for me, but managed to work it all out with some crosses. And was almost done before I finally had the trick dawn on me. Clever theme and a nice puzzle. And... puzzle find today - A Sunday from November 29, 1987 by Bert H. Kruse with the title "Not So Fast!" Some theme answers: AMBLEOFTHEMILL MTMOSEYMORE POKYPUDDING OTORTOISETOWER SAUNTERCODE STBERNARDBUS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=11/29/1987&h=13d ....

Fun puzzle without A DOUT!

And having four intersections of theme material is incredible.

Like many here I've never heard MADS used that way, but it doesn't SADDs me.

I was assured yesterday that I could comment on bad clues here and not be accosted. I trust the same will be true of my issues with some answers, even thought my experience says otherwise. 

Some REALLY awkward answers here. ASKTO, SADD crossing IONIA, OSS, MADS — all extremely clunky. The theme too, as usual, was pretty inane and useless.

Not a bad debut but could have used some tweaking.
Crossword Puzzle keeps shutting down and reverting to home game page while I’m playing. Bug?

You seldom see long theme answers occurring in both the Acrosses and Downs -- so even though I don't make grids, I know it's a lot harder to accomplish.  There's really nice density of theme here, and two of the choices, MAGIC TOUCH and MEAL TICKET, are colorful answers. And although it's a familiar category of early week theme types, the fact that it's phonetic rather than literal makes it more interesting than it would otherwise be.

At 38A: the best clue for AD OUT I've ever seen.

Re 35D:  Is the reason that I'm not a powerful person who has held positions of real power in either industry or government because I lack the ability to POWERNAP?  I had never even heard the term, much less ever accomplished it.  But I can picture all the powerful people I've ever met taking a POWERNAP.  And then I can picture them springing up from their POWERNAP completely refreshed and ready to accumulate more power.  I, OTOH, wake up from the rare naps I manage to accomplish groggy and grumpy and ready to go back to sleep.

Re FRAIL for "easily broken" -- I wrote "FRAIL -- HANDLE WITH CARE!"  on a shipment of fine handblown glassware, and they sent the package to the hospital.  I think maybe you meant "fragile", John?

A nicely executed puzzle with no junky fill.
This was a fun puzzle. Thanks.

Ah, there you are. Page Not Found has been found at last.
This took twice as long as yesterday’s offering. Could it be that Saturday will take most of the day? Hope not.
There seemed to be a lot of acronyms here that felt a tad clunky, but the theme was amusing.
ALE GLASSES. Never, in my umpteen years of bar work have I used that term. Pint, jug, sleeve, straight, pilsner, yep. They are indeed all glasses intended to hold ale. But, ‘a pint of your best in an ALE GLASS’  has never been uttered in my hearing.
I loved this puzzle, especially the clue to adout! Way to go!

Nice to see Mr Toad in the puzzle, along with the other MT clues. But oh man can we get a break from the obscure tennis terms? (Obscure to anyone who doesn't play or watch tennis.) Adout indeed. Also was confused about being caught inalie. (I got it now)

If I spent 10 minutes to solve, but 7 minutes after the solve staring at the theme entries to finally understand the theme. [headslap]

What is my solve time?

Asking for a friend…

Loved this one!!! Both Monday and Tuesday this week were sharp and clever. Congrats on a great first construction. 

Mr. Toad brings back such fond memories of losing myself in the gorgeous world of Kenneth Grahame.
Starting yesterday, the NYT Games app began crashing every couple of minutes on my IOS device. I usually play on wi fi, but I'm on mobile data for a couple of days while travelling.

So many opposite reactions to this debut! (More than usual? Maybe not...)

For me, it was a fun puzzle. Easier for me than yesterday, possibly because I had the time to sit and do it all at once. Yesterday, I had to do it in dribs and drabs, due to work and other unnecessary bothers. 

When there is a word I'm unfamiliar with, I work the opposite direction, and the other words in the area, until I can close in to just a few letters empty. I don't get bent out of shape because it's unfamiliar to me. The creator does not know what each of us knows. I file the new word in my mental crossword filing cabinet. I've never heard the term ADOUT before, but it's no longer new to me. 

Like Nancy, a POWERNAP leaves me groggy, but the older I get, the more I need one. I have a lounge chair in my study that I'd like to replace with one of those recliners that helps you stand. Meanwhile my husband calls it the "sleep study" because if he sits in that chair, yes, you guessed it, he falls asleep.

Perfect puzzle. MEALTICKET. ORDERIN. POWERNAP. What else do you need? Leave the MACHINETOOLS in the garage. One and done. No lookups. Three-day streak. Yea! The Wordplay was delayed. I was thinking maybe the writer got caught in MassTransit on the MTa or needed an eMT. Maybe there was a MajorTrauma. Maybe the writer would have communicated with us by MentalTelepathy. But no. It's just a puzzle that is MightyTempting. Better than a MechanicalToy. And MuchoTerrific. Even MaybeToo much. Well, ManyThanks. And here's a MusicalTreat. Somehow I feel that everyone must know these amazing songs from yore, but probably not. This one is also in my DNA. 19A [*Knack for success] From The Platters You've got the magic touch It makes me glow so much It casts a spell, it rings a bell The magic touch https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hHiW7AUUO-I

This is a straightforward puzzle that I have no big complaints with. It did feel clunky though. Like the punchline of a joke that I guess works but doesn't seem natural? 
Anyway, perfectly cromulent Tuesday. Not a joy, though.
Great debut, John!  Congratulations.
Funday Tuesday! Thank you!

Fun puzzle. Although I have to admit I didn’t “get” the theme until I read the Column. 

Rating:
8/10
Anybody else put brownBEAR first in the Midi today, read the result of the circled (odd) letters, and back track *real* fast with a wide-eyed “OMG nope, that’s gotta be BLACKBEAR”? Or was it just me…

Why do I get this every time I check my puzzle? Used to only ask the first time - now every time? 

“You can reveal or check a part of this puzzle, but it will exclude this puzzle from a streak. Check anyway / Never Mind”.

It's always good to see a reference to Wind in the Willows. It reminds me of my earlier life messin' about in boats. 

MISTER TOAD was a bit of a TOAD, though. I rather preferred rat.
The crossword, Midi Crossword and the Mini Crossword time out in 2 minutes 30 seconds. I have rebooted my iPad and logged out of the NYT and re logged in.

poem made from words in today’s puzzle
a/ the wreckless wind rolled in a tailgating party breaking the broken tree raising the river d/ suddenly soooey! piggy! piggy! piggy! piggy! piggy! p i g g y ! ! here cease all a/ to all to all all in all out to empty
Easy solve, but the theme doesn't make sense.
TIL - ADOUT (AD-OUT).  Need to brush up on my tennis terms.

My NYT games app started shutting down unexpectedly last night. I can’t seem to get through a puzzle without being knocked out. I’m using the iOS version. I rebooted, did hard shut down, updated the iOS software, and reinstalled the app. Nothing works. Can you help?
MidasTOUCH and Advil ruined my top left 🫣
My game keeps on crashing please fix this bug

Dunno why you reordered the games in the app, but was willing to roll with it. But the crossword crashed out the whole game app several times, so I’ll mention that. 

Shoddy work. Not sure why anything is above the Crosswords, but I could understand Wordle being up there. But Connections? Does that have more players than the Crosswords? I would guess not. Spelling Bee has a lot more players based on the forums. Connections isn’t even a NYT original or big purchase. It’s a rip of a British game show.

I liked it! I mean, I, like, liked it! If it were about a boy, you would have to ask if I, like, like him... or if I, like, like him like him. 

Oh, wrong day. Sorry! But I genuinely enjoyed this! I love a big theme, so the four crossing entries did not leave me MT at all! And actually, the theme did help me with MACHINETOOLS. I knew they were tools, but wasn't totally sure what kind they were. 

I also liked seeing DONORS in there... I know it wasn't a particular fun clue or anything, but I spend much of my time and energy thinking about DONORS and how to help them see what a difference their philanthropy makes in our world. I do love my job!! 

As you may recall, I also love my martinis, but never - ever! - DIRTY! I want them as clean as humanly possible. Like, don't even think about olives while you're making it... don't look at them, don't acknowledge their existence in any way! 

Still, I will raise my very clean martini to our constructor from Mad-town, WI—hopefully with a freshly grilled beer BRAT!!  Oh yeah!  🍸🌭🍸

Clever and interesting, especially for a first effort. 
I had 'ghosts' for a long time. instead of GO DARK, until it became obvious it wasn't working. 
Enjoyable.
Why does the “Check Puzzle Now” warning keeps popping up every time I want to use it while it didn’t previously?
Solved the whole puzzle, with enjoyment, and then stared blankly at the revealer and starred clues,,,and I do mean blankly.  I hope it’s the heat and not senescence!
BTW, the crossword has been quitting on me randomly recently. More or less once or twice per game. Anyone else experiencing this?
Update:  it looks like the bug many people have reported is restricted to the iPad.  i just did an archive puzzle on my phone with no problems.

Congratulations on a terrific debut, John Liber. It was indeed a very enjoyable solve, with just the right amount of crunch for me. Can't wait to see your next offering.
Like Sam, I had never heard or seen MADS used as a verb, so that was about my last fill. I'll try to remember that usage. If it's really "old-style" I suppose I should have known it, as I am quite old. So I might have trouble remembering it. :-)
Constant crashing on the iPad
Not my brain skipping straight to Medieval history for "Normandy" and wondering what William the Conqueror had to do with D-Day...
Boo to moving the crosswords down to the bottom of the games app display

A question for the columnist: I know next to nothing about wrestling, but isn't a Technical Knockout (TKO) from boxing? I've never encountered that term in relation to wrestling (but I have heard of a body SLAM). But maybe Sam knows more about wrestling than she realizes?
Nice debut, John Liber!   And a shout-out to my hometown Madison.   Can't be MADS about that.

I'm going to take a chance here.

I once taught at a university in Tennessee whose rival was Middle Tennessee State University.

A fine university in Murfreesboro with whom I have no quarrel whatsoever.  However...

Those of us at the rival university used to joke about their football helmets having the logo MT.  

We'd laugh about that and they we'd play them in football and they'd kill us.

Question for all you folks reporting that the app isn't working...can you tell me why you use it? Does it usually improve the experience? I've read so many complaints about it over the years that I never wanted it ( and rejoiced when the site stopped trying to get me to install it).
I’m trying to do the NYT crossword and the app keeps closing! It’s been doing this for two days. What is the problem. And yes I updated the app!!!
I solved the puzzle but had to come to the column to understand the theme.

I think that clue for 29.A, "Do more work on, as a farrier might" is misleading. The phrase, "as... might" makes me think I should look for a generalized word, which maybe applies to a farrier in particular. But "RESHOE"? I've never used "reshoe" to mean doing more work on anything, like a term paper, or a shop project. Nope. And "ALEGLASS"? There's a specific glass for ales? Bah, humbug. Show me one. I dare ya. In fact...I double dog dare ya.

One more puzzle find. Might re-post this tomorrow. A Thursday from June 15, 1995 by Norma Steinberg. Theme answers in that one: MTPROMISES MEWINNERS JAMESAG KSERASERA EZCOMEEZGO Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/15/1995&h=17a ....

The crashing glitch appears to be fixed. YMMV
New update on the NYT Games app rolled out a short while ago. 
I did today’s puzzle, as well well as several in the archive. All good on my end 🥂
Mix Tape, and for all of us (not many here probably) who use the ENDF files for nuclear properties, Material Tape.  Like elastic interactions.
WOW! Outstanding debut, Mr. Liber!! Very clever construction that made me work for my Tuesday solve while chuckling along the way at several of your entries. Well done!😊
Can anyone explain the midi clue about the circled numbers?
Does anybody say Brat when referring to bratwurst? Or when referring to hot dogs? I’ve never heard that

@Geoff Offermann

17 times in 17 minutes.

@Geoff Offermann
Glad to know it’s not just me
Ditto.  On my iPad,  Are there problems on other platforms?
@Doug, same here.

@Geoff Offermann


Same. Shoulda looked at the comments before writing my own. Weird. Wonder if they updated and “improved” it?  😬
@Geoff Offermann it started yesterday 6/29 for me. Right after app update. I’m on iOS.

@Geoff Offermann ditto. Email the customer support, hopefully they will have a fix soon if there are enough emails. 
Ironically, it crashed again as I was typing this email.
We give up: which one?

@Wendy Laubach

ADOUT?

@HfA

Think tennis

@NYC Traveler

Nope.

@Barry Ancona

7:36 am and still not open.

@PB
same happened to me tonight  (iPhone 14).   I updated the NYT Games app just before it started happening.    Noticed the phone was getting hot as well (related?)
@PB me too. Like four times. Updated the app and it’s still happening. 😡

@PB

Just by luck, I was solving on my desktop--no app involved--for the first time in two and a half weeks.  No issues.
@PB me too! Glad to know it’s not just me.  Haven’t discovered a cure yet though.
@SP, I never heard of ale glasses before, but apparently they are a thing: https://www.crateandbarrel.com/ideas-and-advice/types-of-beer-glasses
@SP - I raise an ale glass to my fellow Wisconsinites at the Memorial Union! (On UW campus for non-Badgers.)

@Cat Lady Margaret I believe your wink count is influenced by your Z-score, so if your sleep contains more 💤 , you may not achieve the full forty winks. 

 Perhaps as a Cat Lady you might be able to weigh in on the important question of the difference between a power nap and a cat nap? I believe a power nap is 10 or 15 minutes— 20 at the absolute max. And it is designed for results. A cat nap feels a little more leisurely, perhaps 20-30? Is this right? Which one gets you to the land of nod, and which one leads you into the arms of Morpheus?

@Petrol
It’s been my observation that a cat nap may last much of the day

@Cat Lady Margaret
Oh god, now it’s competitive napping!  I’m sure that’s already a thing.

@Petrol

Purrfectly reasonable questions.

@RL

The umpire or announcer would say “ADOUT” as a warning or notice, not “Advantage Out” even though that’s what it’s short for.

I hope the NYT continues to *not* bother to specify "abbreviation" or "informal", etc., when an answer is the main way that people refer to something. As a tennis player (before aging out) and spectator, I have heard AD OUT far, far more often than the long version.
At Wimbledon today (okay, via ESPN) I heard the umpire often say in a booming voice "Advantage, Djokovic" or "Advantage, Kecmanic".

@Dan fair point. However we tend to get abbreviated cluing for several words whose abbreviated form is more commonly used, for example, the more commonly used “KO” v the crossword constructors “KAYO.” The commonality of abbreviated usage does not always seem to translate into non-abbreviated cluing.

@Dan

Good point, I thought of that right after I posted. But still it’s such a common phrase, and something that most casual tennis players say if they are announcing the score themselves before serving, that it’s still fair game.

@RL

No.

ADOUT, while it is an abbreviation, is the preferred conversational term.  Like "movie" instead of "moving picture" and "flu" for "influenza."

At some point the shortened forms become the common parlance and anyone using the original form will be looked at kinda sideways.

So, no "for short" or "in brief" required.

@SBK in TO - Whatever the umpire says (see other replies), the scoreboard and scores shown on the tv screen say AD OUT and Ad In.  Crossword answer is valid.      You will see this at the Wimbledon tournament starting this week.

@Matt Correct. And so many words that are shortenings are so widely used over their original forms that many people don't even realized that they are, or know what the full term is: decal, recap, budgie (the bird). And eventually, shortenings make it to the dictionary without an abbreviation marker; they are now words unto themselves: https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/ad-out And in case you didn't know: decalcomania, recapitulation, budgerigar.
@Steve L Thanks for the decalcomania word. Never heard of it. It has kind of a fascinating etymology.

@Dan
It just got fixed here at 6:00 am PT.

@Red Carpet
Sincere question. Why do you associate Julia Stiles with Valley Talk? Nothing that I've read about her,  heard from her or any performance of hers that I've seen indicates Valley-ish to me.

@Vaer

She brings to mind the same time frame as valley talk, to me at least, so maybe that's what they meant.

@Vaer

Same era of movie watching

@Matt  Thanks - that's saved me resorting to Google.  As a 60-something Brit I've only ever (very occasionally) dipped into Wimbledon, so I've only ever heard "Advantage Miss Wade" or "Advantage Murray".  I've only ever come across ADOUT in the last few years since I've been doing the NYT crossword.

@Chris

Of course, it's actually AD OUT.  And the officials give the name of the player, not "in" or "out," but the commentators may, and in informal games, the players themselves call it that way.

@Matt Thank you. I googled it and couldn't find a definition that made sense. Of course, Google thought I meant ABOUT. But the definition for ADOUT was "noun. young knight, warrior, or heroic youth, especially in medieval or chivalric stories." It made zero sense.
@Dorothy  I had to google it after I got the happy music. I'm not sure it belongs in a Tuesday puzzle.

@Petrol

I'm amazed by this.  Not only can I not recall instantly without hint words I need for the puzzle, I also can't imagine there wouldn't be one across--a proper name, a river, a clue that makes no sense--that would be the end of the game.

@Francis Well, you ride your luck to a certain extent. I wasn't sure if the anti-trafficking agency was DEA or ATF, and I didn't get ADOUT for a while, which made me wonder if I had a mistake. I also initially guessed POWERsup instead of POWERNAP, which slowed me down, until IONIA set me right. There were so many gimmes today, and no duff clues at all, so it all fell into place. Try it next Monday!

@Petrol,
My hat is off to you and others who attempt this (and sometimes succeed!) 
I tried doing this on a super easy puzzle (not NYT) yesterday (thanks to you). I still got stumped when one of the across clues was simply, “see 22D”. 

Interesting, I was able to get the down word by letter pattern, but the across stymied me, and it was that lone word that tripped me up. 

I do think I will attempt this next week on the early week puzzles. Builds a skill (recognizing letter patterns) that is helpful for solving any crossword.

@Bill
Thomas Hardy  -- Far From the Madding Crowd

@Vaer
I thought of that too--although obviously derived from the verb "mad," "madding" has its own life (and dictionary entry) as an adjective. In any case, Tom Hardy was quoting Tom Gray, writing about 1750. BTW, the OED also cites "madding" as a verb, meaning about the same thing, but when people use it that way, it really mads me.

@Times Rita
Maybe back then it wasn't possible to change one's location or change it easily?

@Xword Junkie

I would have preferred a reference to MADS Mikkelson (the baddie in "Casino Royale") to the clue as given.

CCNY,
I don't think your call to Natick will go through using a PnONE.

@Barry Ancona

Oh Barry.
Pleeeease tell me you knew I was kidding.

Pnone?
SRSLY?

@Barry Ancona

Oh, wait. 

Someone didn’t get the joke.

I thought it was you. 

‘Twas CC.

CC is a bit slow today,
and reading way too fast…

@Mean Old Lady

Why is Dear Hubby against GM vehicles?
@Robert C. Not mine.

@Ιασων I must be in a test group. Will the editor who approves this comment please tell the dev team to undo this? I don’t want to have to scroll past wordle, connections, spelling bee, AND pips to get to the crossword. That’s ridiculous. Give me the option to reorder those tiles if you want wordle at the top
@Ιασων and sudoku and Crossplay

@Robert C. Android, but mine is like that too. I really wish we could customize the order of the games. I only do the main crossword, wordle, and connections. Scrolling through all the others to get to this one is annoying.
@Jim The order on mine (iPhone and iPad) have changed too
@D mad?

@D
I agree that some of the fill was a little weird, but entries like ASK TO are pretty common early-week fill--would you prefer [Mauryan dynasty emperor (var.)]? And Ionia is not only a place in Greece, but a city in Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Missouri, Nebraska, and *two* distinct hamlets in New York. And, all snark aside, any teen who has lost a classmate in a fatal car crash, may take offense to your dismissal of SADD.
MADS is a little iffier, but I was really looking forward to all the hullabaloo it is going to cause:-)

Does this constitute accosting?

@D https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Students_Against_Destructive_Decisions (Formerly Students Against Drunk Driving, after Mothers Against Drunk Driving.) Do they also have Mothers and Students Against Texting, etc., While Driving?

@D

“ The theme too, as usual, was pretty inane and useless.”

When is a theme ever useful?
I’m convinced this is a professional troll job.
@Weak Sometimes the theme helps solve the puzzle or is at least interesting. This did/was neither.

@Bob T.

Nice find. 
You are overthinking it. 

It will be ION. 
The clue will be “charged particle”. 
Or 
“four letter animal whose first letter is L”

@Rob @mirle234 I finally got "ADOUT" (or "AD OUT"). It's an older tennis term for the point after DEUCE (40-40) where the person not serving has the advantage. I don't think they use anymore and instead say "Advantage ".

@Bradley I guess it's still called "ad out" but they always just say: Advantage "the receiver" now on broadcasts. https://www.usta.com/en/home/improve/tips-and-instruction/national/tennis-scoring-rules.html
